stem:
A 64-year-old man is brought to a metropolitan emergency department by paramedics with right-sided weakness and expressive aphasia first noticed by his wife when he woke at 06:30. He last spoke normally at 22:00 the night before. He arrives at 07:40. He has hypertension and atrial fibrillation; he takes apixaban 5 mg twice daily, last dose 18:00 yesterday. National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale (NIHSS) is 14.

Non-contrast computed tomography (CT) brain shows no haemorrhage and no early ischaemic change. CT perfusion shows a 12 mL ischaemic core and 78 mL of penumbra with mismatch ratio 7.5.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88 irregular, BP 168/92, SpO2 97% room air, RR 16, BSL 6.4 mmol/L.
data_table.Imaging: Non-contrast CT no haemorrhage. CTP core 12 mL, penumbra 78 mL, mismatch 7.5.
data_table.Medications: Apixaban 5 mg BD, last dose 18:00 prior day.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate reperfusion strategy?

options.text: Intravenous alteplase 0.9 mg/kg now
options.rationale: Apixaban within 48 hours is a contraindication to intravenous thrombolysis unless a specific reversal pathway and DOAC level <30 ng/mL are documented. Last dose 18:00 yesterday is approximately 14 hours ago (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Intravenous tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg bolus then endovascular thrombectomy
options.rationale: Lytic therapy is contraindicated by recent apixaban. Bridging lysis would only apply to a thrombolysis-eligible patient (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Endovascular thrombectomy without prior thrombolysis
options.rationale: Large vessel occlusion is implied by NIHSS 14 with favourable perfusion mismatch within an extended window. Thrombectomy alone is appropriate when lytics are contraindicated (recent DOAC), and benefit extends to 24 hours when CTP mismatch supports it (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022; DAWN/DEFUSE-3 criteria).

options.text: Aspirin 300 mg orally and admit to stroke unit
options.rationale: Antiplatelet alone is the default only when reperfusion therapy is not available or not indicated; large vessel occlusion with salvageable penumbra mandates thrombectomy referral (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Andexanet alfa reversal then intravenous alteplase
options.rationale: Andexanet is not approved in Australia for facilitating thrombolysis and induces a prothrombotic state which may worsen stroke outcomes. Reversal-then-lysis is not a Stroke Foundation pathway (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022; TGA product information).

explanation.summary: When stroke onset is unwitnessed but last-known-well is >4.5 hours, eligibility for reperfusion depends on imaging selection. Recent DOAC use within 48 hours contraindicates intravenous thrombolysis, but endovascular thrombectomy remains available out to 24 hours when CT perfusion shows favourable mismatch.
key_points: Use last-known-well, not symptom-discovery time, as t=0
DOAC within 48 h = lytic contraindication unless level <30 ng/mL documented
Thrombectomy window extends to 24 h with CTP mismatch (DAWN/DEFUSE-3)
BP must be <185/110 prior to lysis, <180/105 post; less strict pre-thrombectomy
explanation.pearls: Thrombolysis exclusion does not automatically exclude thrombectomy - the two pathways have different eligibility criteria.

stem: A 58-year-old woman presents 90 minutes after sudden-onset left hemiparesis and dysarthria witnessed at work. NIHSS is 9. Non-contrast CT brain shows no haemorrhage. Blood pressure on three measurements 5 minutes apart is 198/112, 196/108 and 194/106. She has no other contraindications to intravenous thrombolysis.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78, BP 196/108 mean of three, SpO2 98% room air.
data_table.NIHSS: 9 (left arm 3, left leg 2, facial 1, dysarthria 2, sensory 1).
data_table.Imaging: Non-contrast CT no haemorrhage, no early ischaemic change.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Proceed with alteplase as benefit outweighs the BP risk
options.rationale: Pre-lysis BP must be reduced to and sustained at <185/110 before alteplase administration; ignoring this raises symptomatic intracranial haemorrhage risk (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Intravenous labetalol 10 to 20 mg bolus, recheck, then alteplase if BP <185/110
options.rationale: Labetalol bolus titration is the Stroke Foundation first-line agent to bring pre-lysis BP under 185/110; alteplase may proceed once target achieved within the time window. Post-lysis target is <180/105 for 24 hours (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022; eTG Neurology).

options.text: Sublingual nifedipine 10 mg and reassess in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine produces unpredictable hypotensive drops that risk extending the infarct and is contraindicated in acute stroke BP management (eTG Cardiovascular).

options.text: Withhold thrombolysis and treat as ineligible due to severe hypertension
options.rationale: Severe hypertension is a relative not absolute exclusion; pharmacologic reduction within the lysis time window is the standard approach when other criteria are met (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Intravenous glyceryl trinitrate infusion at 5 mcg/min titrated to BP <140/90
options.rationale: Aggressive reduction below 140/90 in acute ischaemic stroke risks expanding the penumbra into core. Target is <185/110 pre-lysis only (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

explanation.summary: Pre-thrombolysis blood pressure target is <185/110 mmHg; post-lysis target is <180/105 mmHg for 24 hours. Labetalol bolus is first-line in Australia. Sublingual nifedipine and aggressive reductions are harmful in acute ischaemic stroke.
key_points: Pre-lysis: BP <185/110 sustained
Post-lysis: BP <180/105 for 24 hours
Labetalol 10-20 mg IV bolus, repeat or infuse as needed
Avoid sublingual nifedipine, avoid over-aggressive lowering
explanation.pearls: Permissive hypertension applies outside the lysis pathway; lysis itself demands tight pre/post BP control to limit haemorrhagic conversion.

stem: A 71-year-old woman presents to a regional hospital 2 hours after sudden right hemiparesis and global aphasia. NIHSS is 18. Non-contrast CT brain shows hyperdense left middle cerebral artery (MCA) sign and an ASPECTS score of 8. The nearest comprehensive stroke centre with endovascular thrombectomy is 90 minutes away by helicopter retrieval; her local hospital does intravenous thrombolysis only.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 84 regular, BP 158/82, SpO2 98% room air, BSL 5.6 mmol/L.
data_table.Imaging: Hyperdense left MCA, ASPECTS 8, no haemorrhage.
data_table.NIHSS: 18 (motor 6, aphasia 3, gaze 2, sensory 2, neglect 2, facial 1, dysarthria 1, level of consciousness 1).

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Administer alteplase locally and arrange urgent retrieval for thrombectomy
options.rationale: The drip-and-ship pathway is recommended when a patient meets thrombolysis criteria at a non-thrombectomy centre and has features suggesting large vessel occlusion (NIHSS >=6, hyperdense MCA, ASPECTS >=6). Lysis is given immediately; retrieval runs in parallel (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Aspirin 300 mg and admit locally without retrieval
options.rationale: Antiplatelet alone forgoes reperfusion benefit in a candidate for both lysis and thrombectomy. NIHSS 18 with ASPECTS 8 is a high-benefit thrombectomy profile (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Direct retrieval to thrombectomy centre, withholding alteplase until arrival
options.rationale: Withholding lysis to await thrombectomy is not supported when local lysis is available; bridging therapy improves outcomes in randomised trials and is the Stroke Foundation recommendation (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Decompressive hemicraniectomy referral
options.rationale: Hemicraniectomy is a consideration for malignant MCA syndrome at 48 hours when there is failure of medical management and oedema; it is not the immediate reperfusion strategy (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Heparin infusion targeting aPTT 60 to 80 seconds
options.rationale: Therapeutic heparin in acute ischaemic stroke increases haemorrhagic conversion without reducing recurrent stroke and is not indicated (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

explanation.summary: Drip-and-ship integrates local lysis with retrieval for endovascular thrombectomy. Criteria suggesting large vessel occlusion include NIHSS >=6, hyperdense vessel sign, and preserved ASPECTS (>=6). Lysis should not be delayed for transfer.
key_points: NIHSS >=6 + suspicion of LVO = thrombectomy consideration
ASPECTS >=6 supports reperfusion benefit
Bridging lysis + thrombectomy beats thrombectomy alone in eligible patients
Therapeutic heparin not indicated in acute ischaemic stroke
explanation.pearls: ASPECTS scores 1 point for each affected MCA territory subdivided; score <=5 suggests established infarct with reduced reperfusion benefit.

stem: A 68-year-old man received intravenous alteplase 2 hours ago for acute ischaemic stroke. NIHSS was 11 pre-lysis, improved to 6 at 1 hour. He now has acute deterioration with NIHSS rising to 19, new vomiting, BP 220/118, HR 52, and reduced level of consciousness. He is on the stroke unit.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 220/118, HR 52, SpO2 94% room air, GCS 11.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Stop any infusing alteplase, urgent non-contrast CT brain, cross-match and prepare cryoprecipitate
options.rationale: Acute neurological deterioration after alteplase with Cushing-like response (hypertension + bradycardia + falling GCS) is symptomatic intracranial haemorrhage until proven otherwise. Stop lysis, image urgently, and prepare reversal with cryoprecipitate 10 units (or fibrinogen concentrate) targeting fibrinogen >1.5 g/L (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Intravenous mannitol 1 g/kg and reassess in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Osmotherapy treats raised intracranial pressure but does not address the precipitating haemorrhage; imaging and reversal must precede mannitol if haemorrhagic conversion is suspected (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Increase alteplase rate to complete remaining dose faster
options.rationale: Continuing or accelerating lysis during deterioration is contraindicated; this is the classic anti-pattern of attributing deterioration to under-treatment (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Aspirin 300 mg and antiemetic
options.rationale: Antiplatelet during suspected haemorrhagic conversion worsens bleeding; antiemetic addresses symptom not cause (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) 50 units/kg
options.rationale: PCC reverses warfarin and factor Xa inhibitors but is not the primary lytic reversal; alteplase reversal targets fibrinogen depletion with cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022; ANZSBT).

explanation.summary: Symptomatic intracranial haemorrhage complicates around 2 to 6% of alteplase-treated strokes. Sudden deterioration with rising BP, falling HR, and reduced GCS demands stop-image-reverse sequence. Reversal targets fibrinogen with cryoprecipitate.
key_points: Cushing reflex + falling GCS post-lysis = haemorrhage until imaged
Stop lytic infusion immediately
Cryoprecipitate 10 units targeting fibrinogen >1.5 g/L
Tranexamic acid is adjunctive; consult haematology
explanation.pearls: Worsening NIHSS by >=4 points in the first 24 hours post-lysis is the trigger for repeat imaging.

stem: A 56-year-old woman wakes at 06:00 with left-sided weakness. She last spoke normally at 23:00 the previous night. She arrives at the emergency department at 06:45. NIHSS is 12. Non-contrast CT brain at 07:00 shows no haemorrhage and no early ischaemic change.

data_table.Timeline: Last known well 23:00; symptom discovery 06:00; arrival 06:45; CT 07:00.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next investigation to determine thrombolysis eligibility?

options.text: Magnetic resonance imaging brain with diffusion-weighted imaging and FLAIR
options.rationale: In wake-up stroke beyond 4.5 hours of last-known-well, MRI DWI-FLAIR mismatch (lesion visible on DWI but not on FLAIR) identifies patients within the metabolic 4.5-hour window who benefit from lysis (WAKE-UP trial; Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Repeat non-contrast CT in 4 hours
options.rationale: Serial CT delays decision-making; the time-window question is best resolved by MRI mismatch or CT perfusion now, not waiting for CT changes to evolve (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Carotid Doppler ultrasound
options.rationale: Carotid Doppler informs secondary prevention but not acute reperfusion eligibility (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Computed tomography venogram
options.rationale: CT venography evaluates cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, not arterial ischaemic stroke time-of-onset (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Electroencephalogram
options.rationale: EEG screens for non-convulsive seizure as a stroke mimic but is not the next step when imaging mismatch can clarify reperfusion eligibility (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

explanation.summary: Wake-up stroke with unknown onset beyond 4.5 hours can still be treated with thrombolysis when MRI DWI-FLAIR mismatch confirms the lesion is metabolically <4.5 hours old. CT perfusion mismatch is an alternative in centres without rapid MRI.
key_points: DWI lesion + no FLAIR signal = <4.5 hours metabolic age
Applies when last-known-well exceeds the conventional time window
WAKE-UP trial supports lysis in selected wake-up strokes
CTP mismatch is the alternative selection tool
explanation.pearls: MRI DWI-FLAIR mismatch is the imaging equivalent of a clock when the actual clock is unknown.

stem: A 34-year-old man with known epilepsy is brought to the emergency department by ambulance. He has been seizing continuously for 8 minutes with generalised tonic-clonic activity. Intravenous access is in place. He weighs approximately 80 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132, BP 168/96, SpO2 91% room air, T 37.6 C, BSL 5.8 mmol/L.
data_table.Airway: Bag-valve-mask, jaw thrust, oral suction in use.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line pharmacological treatment?

options.text: Midazolam 10 mg intramuscular
options.rationale: Intramuscular midazolam is appropriate when intravenous access is unavailable; with IV access in place the intravenous benzodiazepine route is preferred (eTG Neurology; ESETT).

options.text: Lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg intravenous (maximum 4 mg)
options.rationale: Lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV (commonly 4 mg) is the first-line benzodiazepine in convulsive status epilepticus when IV access is available, per Therapeutic Guidelines Neurology. Diazepam is an acceptable alternative; midazolam IM is for the no-IV scenario (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Phenytoin 20 mg/kg intravenous infusion
options.rationale: Phenytoin is a second-line agent given after benzodiazepine failure, not first-line (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg intravenous infusion
options.rationale: Levetiracetam is a second-line option after benzodiazepine failure per ESETT, not the initial agent (eTG Neurology; ESETT trial).

options.text: Propofol 2 mg/kg intravenous bolus and intubation
options.rationale: Propofol induction is reserved for refractory status epilepticus after first- and second-line agents have failed (eTG Neurology).

explanation.summary: Convulsive status epilepticus (seizure >5 minutes or recurrent without recovery) is treated stepwise: first-line benzodiazepine, second-line antiepileptic, then anaesthetic for refractory status. IV lorazepam is preferred when IV access is available; IM midazolam when not.
key_points: First-line: IV lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg, IV diazepam 0.15 mg/kg, or IM midazolam 10 mg
Second-line: levetiracetam, phenytoin/fosphenytoin, or sodium valproate
Refractory (after two lines): anaesthetic induction with intubation
Check glucose, electrolytes, pregnancy test in parallel
explanation.pearls: Underdosing the benzodiazepine is the most common error; give the full weight-based dose, not a cautious half-dose.

subtopic_detail: Second-line agent selection after benzodiazepine failure
stem: A 28-year-old woman has been seizing for 18 minutes. She received two doses of intravenous midazolam 5 mg, 5 minutes apart, with no cessation. She has no known epilepsy, is 16 weeks pregnant, and weighs 65 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 140, BP 156/94, SpO2 88% on 15 L non-rebreather, T 38.2 C, BSL 6.2 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next pharmacological agent?

options.text: Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg intravenous over 10 minutes
options.rationale: Levetiracetam 40 to 60 mg/kg IV is the preferred second-line agent in pregnancy due to absence of teratogenicity in trimester comparisons, and showed equivalence to phenytoin and valproate in ESETT. Sodium valproate is teratogenic and avoided in pregnancy (eTG Neurology; RANZCOG; ESETT).

options.text: Sodium valproate 40 mg/kg intravenous
options.rationale: Sodium valproate is strongly teratogenic (neural tube defects, neurodevelopmental harm); avoided in pregnancy if alternatives exist (RANZCOG).

options.text: Phenytoin 20 mg/kg intravenous infusion
options.rationale: Phenytoin is acceptable but ranks behind levetiracetam in pregnancy due to foetal hydantoin syndrome risk and the cardiotoxicity risk if infused too rapidly (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Magnesium sulfate 4 g intravenous over 20 minutes
options.rationale: Magnesium sulfate is first-line for eclampsia at >=20 weeks gestation; at 16 weeks pre-eclampsia is uncommon and her presentation lacks proteinuria, hypertension, or headache to indicate eclampsia (RANZCOG eclampsia).

options.text: Repeat midazolam 5 mg intravenous
options.rationale: After two adequate benzodiazepine doses without response, repeating the benzodiazepine without escalating is the classic anti-pattern; move to second-line antiepileptic (eTG Neurology).

explanation.summary: After benzodiazepine failure in convulsive status epilepticus, second-line agents are levetiracetam, phenytoin/fosphenytoin, or sodium valproate. In pregnancy levetiracetam is preferred; valproate is avoided due to teratogenicity. Magnesium is reserved for eclampsia.
key_points: ESETT showed levetiracetam, fosphenytoin and valproate equivalent in non-pregnant adults
Levetiracetam preferred in pregnancy
Valproate avoided in any woman of reproductive potential without contraception
Magnesium for eclampsia, not generic seizures
explanation.pearls: Anyone with new-onset seizure in pregnancy >=20 weeks is eclampsia until proven otherwise; check BP, urinalysis, reflexes immediately.

stem: A 45-year-old man has had ongoing seizure activity for 45 minutes despite two doses of intravenous midazolam 10 mg and intravenous levetiracetam 60 mg/kg. He is now in the resuscitation bay with the anaesthetic registrar.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, BP 178/102, SpO2 86% on 15 L non-rebreather.
data_table.Treatments given: Midazolam 10 mg x2 IV. Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV completed.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Rapid sequence induction with propofol and intubation, then propofol or midazolam infusion with continuous EEG
options.rationale: Refractory status epilepticus (failure of first- and second-line) requires anaesthetic-depth induction with intubation, infusion of propofol or midazolam, and continuous EEG to confirm electrographic seizure cessation (eTG Neurology; NEJM refractory status review).

options.text: Add a third antiepileptic (phenytoin 20 mg/kg) and wait 20 minutes
options.rationale: After two failed agents the patient is in refractory status; further sequential boluses delay airway protection and cerebral oxygenation. Anaesthetic management is now indicated (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Therapeutic hypothermia to 33 degrees Celsius
options.rationale: Therapeutic hypothermia is not standard for status epilepticus; evidence does not show seizure benefit and there are coagulation and infection harms (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Ketogenic diet via nasogastric tube
options.rationale: Ketogenic diet is a refractory-status adjunct after days of failed conventional therapy, not the acute step (NHMRC; eTG Neurology).

options.text: Lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid analysis
options.rationale: Workup for cause is essential but must not delay seizure termination; LP follows airway control and seizure cessation (eTG Neurology).

explanation.summary: Refractory status epilepticus (>30 minutes or failure of two agents) requires anaesthetic induction with intubation and continuous EEG monitoring. Propofol or midazolam infusion is titrated to seizure suppression or burst suppression. Cause workup occurs in parallel.
key_points: Refractory = failure of first- and second-line
Super-refractory = failure of anaesthetic agents at 24 hours
Continuous EEG mandatory once anaesthetised (non-convulsive seizures common)
Look for cause: imaging, LP, electrolytes, toxic screen, autoimmune panel
explanation.pearls: Mortality in refractory status approaches 30%; the urgency is termination not diagnosis.

stem: A 42-year-old man presents to a regional hospital with 5 days of progressive ascending lower limb weakness, now involving the proximal upper limbs and mild facial droop. He had gastroenteritis 3 weeks ago. Examination shows symmetrical weakness 3/5 in the legs and 4/5 in the arms with absent ankle and knee reflexes. Cranial nerve examination shows bilateral mild facial weakness. Sensation is intact.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 102, BP 148/96 (was 124/78 on admission), SpO2 96% room air, RR 22.
data_table.Examination: Symmetrical LL weakness 3/5, UL 4/5, areflexia, bilateral facial droop, sensation intact.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management priority?

options.text: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days and admission to a monitored bed with respiratory and autonomic surveillance
options.rationale: Guillain-Barre syndrome (GBS) with FVC <20 mL/kg, bulbar involvement, or autonomic dysfunction (her labile BP and tachycardia) requires high-dependency monitoring. IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days or plasma exchange are equivalent first-line treatments (eTG Neurology; ANZAN GBS guidance).

options.text: Methylprednisolone 1 g IV daily for 5 days
options.rationale: Corticosteroids do not improve outcomes in GBS and are not recommended; this distinguishes GBS treatment from CIDP (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy) which does respond to steroids (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Pyridostigmine 60 mg orally four times daily
options.rationale: Pyridostigmine treats myasthenia gravis (presynaptic acetylcholine availability), not GBS; the differential matters because both cause neuromuscular weakness but mechanism differs (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Outpatient nerve conduction studies and review in 1 week
options.rationale: Outpatient management is inappropriate when FVC is <20 mL/kg with bulbar signs; rapid respiratory decompensation is a real risk (ANZAN; eTG Neurology).

options.text: Empirical aciclovir 10 mg/kg IV three times daily
options.rationale: Aciclovir is for HSV encephalitis; GBS is post-infectious autoimmune and does not respond to antivirals (eTG Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: GBS is an acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy classically following Campylobacter, viral GI or respiratory illness. Diagnosis is clinical (ascending symmetric weakness, areflexia) supported by CSF albuminocytological dissociation. First-line treatment is IVIG or plasma exchange; steroids do not work.
key_points: Ascending symmetric weakness + areflexia = GBS until proven otherwise
FVC <20 mL/kg, bulbar signs, autonomic instability = high-dependency
IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days OR plasma exchange (equivalent)
Steroids do not work in GBS (key distinction from CIDP)
explanation.pearls: Serial FVC every 4-6 hours; intubate when FVC <15 mL/kg, negative inspiratory force less negative than -25 cmH2O, or bulbar failure.

stem: A 38-year-old woman presents with 3 weeks of intermittent diplopia worse in the evenings, fatigable proximal limb weakness, and dysphagia for solids that worsens through a meal. Reflexes are preserved. There is no sensory disturbance and no recent viral or gastrointestinal illness. Ice pack applied to the right eyelid for 2 minutes improves ptosis.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78, BP 124/72, SpO2 99% room air, RR 14, FVC 42 mL/kg.
data_table.Examination: Fatigable proximal weakness, preserved reflexes, no sensory level, positive ice pack test.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate confirmatory investigation?

options.text: Acetylcholine receptor antibody assay
options.rationale: Acetylcholine receptor (AChR) antibodies are positive in 80 to 90% of generalised myasthenia gravis (MG) and are the first-line serological test. MuSK antibodies are checked if AChR is negative. The clinical picture (fatigability, bulbar, ocular, ice pack response, preserved reflexes) is MG not GBS (eTG Neurology; ANZAN MG).

options.text: Lumbar puncture for cytological dissociation
options.rationale: Albuminocytological dissociation supports GBS not MG; this stem lacks ascending weakness, areflexia, or post-infectious context (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Nerve conduction studies for demyelination
options.rationale: Demyelinating features support GBS or CIDP; in MG the diagnostic neurophysiology is repetitive nerve stimulation (decrement) or single-fibre EMG (jitter), not standard NCS (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Stool culture for Campylobacter jejuni
options.rationale: Campylobacter precedes GBS not MG, and there is no GI history (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Magnetic resonance imaging spine
options.rationale: Spine MRI evaluates myelopathy, which would present with sensory level and bladder/bowel involvement, not present here (eTG Neurology).

explanation.summary: Myasthenia gravis is a postsynaptic neuromuscular junction disorder marked by fatigable weakness, ocular and bulbar involvement, and preserved reflexes/sensation. Acetylcholine receptor antibodies are the first-line confirmatory test; MuSK antibodies are checked when AChR is negative.
key_points: MG: fatigable, ocular/bulbar, reflexes preserved, no sensory loss
GBS: ascending, areflexic, post-infectious, CSF protein up
AChR antibodies positive in 80-90% of generalised MG
All MG patients need CT thorax for thymoma
explanation.pearls: Ice pack test (improves ptosis) and rest test are bedside MG screens; both leverage temperature-sensitive AChR function.

stem: A 52-year-old woman with seropositive myasthenia gravis on pyridostigmine 60 mg four times daily and prednisolone 15 mg daily presents to a metropolitan emergency department with 24 hours of worsening dysphagia, breathlessness on lying flat, and inability to lift her head off the pillow. She was started on azithromycin 500 mg by her general practitioner 3 days ago for sinusitis. She is alert but tachypnoeic.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 122, BP 142/86, SpO2 92% on 4 L nasal prongs, RR 28.
data_table.Bedside: FVC 12 mL/kg, negative inspiratory force -18 cmH2O, single breath count 8.
data_table.Examination: Bulbar weakness, neck flexor 2/5, paradoxical abdominal breathing.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management priority?

options.text: Stop azithromycin, transfer to intensive care for likely intubation, and start plasma exchange or intravenous immunoglobulin
options.rationale: Myasthenic crisis with FVC <15 mL/kg, paradoxical breathing, and bulbar failure mandates ICU admission with airway preparedness. Macrolides (including azithromycin), aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, and beta-blockers worsen MG and must be stopped. Plasma exchange or IVIG are first-line acute treatments (eTG Neurology; ANZAN; eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Increase pyridostigmine to 120 mg orally hourly
options.rationale: Over-dosing pyridostigmine causes cholinergic crisis (indistinguishable weakness picture); escalating cholinesterase inhibitor without crisis treatment is dangerous (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Methylprednisolone 1 g IV without stopping azithromycin
options.rationale: High-dose steroid initiation can transiently worsen MG (steroid dip) in the first 1 to 2 weeks; in crisis IVIG/plasma exchange precedes steroid escalation, and the precipitating drug must be stopped (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Edrophonium test to differentiate myasthenic from cholinergic crisis
options.rationale: Edrophonium (Tensilon) testing is largely abandoned due to risks; bedside ventilatory assessment and treatment of crisis take priority (ANZAN).

options.text: Non-invasive ventilation as a long-term plan, no specific MG-directed therapy
options.rationale: NIV may bridge respiratory support short-term but is not a substitute for crisis-directed plasma exchange/IVIG and offending-drug cessation (eTG Neurology; ANZICS).

explanation.summary: Myasthenic crisis is respiratory failure due to MG, precipitated by infection, surgery, pregnancy, or certain drugs (macrolides, aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, telithromycin, beta-blockers, magnesium). ICU admission, plasma exchange or IVIG, and removal of precipitants is the bundle.
key_points: FVC <15 mL/kg or NIF less negative than -25 cmH2O = intubation imminent
Drugs to stop in MG: macrolides, aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers, magnesium
Plasma exchange and IVIG equivalent first-line
Steroid dip can worsen weakness early; start low and observe
explanation.pearls: Single breath count <20 correlates with FVC <1 L; useful when spirometer unavailable.

stem: A 67-year-old woman presents to a regional emergency department with 12 hours of fever, severe headache, neck stiffness and progressive confusion. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus. She has no penicillin allergy. Kernig sign is positive. There are no focal neurological signs and Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) is 13.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 118, BP 102/64, SpO2 96% room air, T 39.2 C, RR 22.
data_table.Examination: Neck stiffness, Kernig positive, GCS 13, no focal signs, no rash.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate empirical antimicrobial regimen?

options.text: Ceftriaxone 2 g intravenous 12-hourly plus benzylpenicillin 2.4 g intravenous 4-hourly plus dexamethasone 10 mg intravenous 6-hourly
options.rationale: Adults >50 years or with comorbidity (diabetes, alcohol misuse, immunocompromise) require Listeria cover, hence benzylpenicillin added to ceftriaxone (or use amoxicillin/ampicillin where stocked). Dexamethasone 10 mg IV 6-hourly given with or just before first antibiotic dose reduces neurological sequelae in pneumococcal meningitis (eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Ceftriaxone 2 g intravenous 12-hourly alone
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone monotherapy is adequate for adults 18 to 50 without comorbidity; at age 67 with diabetes, Listeria cover is mandated (eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Vancomycin 30 mg/kg intravenous loading dose plus meropenem 2 g intravenous 8-hourly
options.rationale: Meropenem with vancomycin is reserved for cephalosporin allergy or institutional resistance concerns; not first-line in straightforward community-acquired adult meningitis (eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Aciclovir 10 mg/kg intravenous 8-hourly alone
options.rationale: Aciclovir treats HSV encephalitis; bacterial meningitis cover takes priority when clinical features suggest bacterial cause. Aciclovir is added when encephalitic features (focal signs, seizures, altered behaviour) coexist (eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Defer antibiotics until lumbar puncture results return
options.rationale: Antibiotics must not wait for CSF; each hour of delay worsens mortality. Blood cultures then immediate empirical antibiotics; LP follows when safe (eTG Antibiotic; CDNA).

explanation.summary: Empirical antibiotics for community-acquired adult bacterial meningitis cover Streptococcus pneumoniae and Neisseria meningitidis with ceftriaxone; adults over 50 or immunocompromised need Listeria cover (benzylpenicillin or amoxicillin). Dexamethasone given with or just before the first antibiotic dose reduces sequelae and mortality.
key_points: Ceftriaxone 2 g IV BD as core cover
Add benzylpenicillin/amoxicillin if >50 or immunocompromised (Listeria)
Dexamethasone 10 mg IV QID with or before first antibiotic
Antibiotics within 1 hour - never wait for LP
explanation.pearls: Continue dexamethasone for 4 days only if CSF confirms pneumococcus; stop if other organism.

stem: A 54-year-old man presents to a tertiary emergency department with fever, headache, neck stiffness and a single focal seizure 1 hour ago. GCS is 12. He has a known history of HIV with CD4 count 180. There are no focal motor signs but he is post-ictal.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110, BP 138/82, SpO2 97% room air, T 38.9 C.
data_table.Examination: Post-ictal, GCS 12, no focal motor deficit, neck stiffness present.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate sequence of immediate steps?

options.text: Blood cultures, empirical antibiotics and dexamethasone, then CT brain before lumbar puncture
options.rationale: Indications for CT before LP include immunocompromise, history of CNS disease, recent seizure, papilloedema, focal neurological signs, or reduced GCS. He has three (immunocompromise, seizure, reduced GCS). Empirical antibiotics and dexamethasone must precede imaging so therapy is not delayed (eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Immediate lumbar puncture without imaging
options.rationale: LP without imaging in this profile risks cerebral herniation if there is mass effect; imaging-first criteria apply (eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: CT brain first, then antibiotics only if CT clear
options.rationale: Withholding antibiotics until after imaging delays therapy and worsens outcomes; antibiotics go in immediately after blood cultures, regardless of imaging plan (eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Magnetic resonance imaging brain first, then lumbar puncture
options.rationale: MRI is slower and unnecessary for the immediate question of mass effect; non-contrast CT is the screening tool for safe LP (eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Treat empirically and avoid lumbar puncture entirely
options.rationale: CSF identifies organism and tailors therapy duration; LP should be performed once imaging excludes mass effect, ideally within 24 hours (eTG Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: Criteria for CT before LP in suspected bacterial meningitis: immunocompromise, history of CNS disease, recent seizure, papilloedema, focal neurology, or reduced GCS. Antibiotics and dexamethasone must not be delayed for imaging.
key_points: Imaging-first criteria: immunocompromise, CNS history, seizure, papilloedema, focal signs, GCS reduced
Antibiotics within 1 h after blood cultures - never wait for imaging or LP
CSF microscopy, culture, PCR, glucose, protein, lactate
Dexamethasone with first antibiotic dose
explanation.pearls: Paired CSF and serum glucose; CSF:serum glucose ratio <0.4 is more specific for bacterial than absolute CSF glucose.

sources.label: Hasbun et al, IDSA practice guideline (Australian alignment via eTG)

stem: A 42-year-old man with suspected bacterial meningitis has received blood cultures and is about to be given his first dose of intravenous ceftriaxone. The registrar asks about dexamethasone timing.

data_table.Plan: Ceftriaxone 2 g IV being drawn up. Blood cultures collected.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate plan for dexamethasone?

options.text: Dexamethasone 10 mg intravenous with or just before the first antibiotic dose, continued 6-hourly for 4 days only if CSF confirms pneumococcus
options.rationale: Dexamethasone reduces mortality and neurological sequelae in pneumococcal meningitis when given with or before the first antibiotic dose. Continue for 4 days if pneumococcus is confirmed; stop if another organism or no CSF growth (eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Dexamethasone 10 mg intravenous after the first antibiotic dose, continued for 10 days regardless of organism
options.rationale: Late steroid administration (after antibiotic) loses the protective effect against TNF release from bacterial lysis. Duration of 10 days exceeds evidence (eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Dexamethasone only after CSF Gram stain confirms organism
options.rationale: Awaiting Gram stain delays steroid past the antibiotic and forfeits benefit; give empirically and de-escalate if non-pneumococcal (eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Hydrocortisone 100 mg intravenous 6-hourly
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone has been studied in septic shock not meningitis; dexamethasone is the evidence-based steroid in bacterial meningitis (eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: No steroids - steroid use is not part of bacterial meningitis management
options.rationale: Adjunctive dexamethasone is part of the recommended bundle for adult community-acquired bacterial meningitis (eTG Antibiotic; de Gans NEJM 2002).

explanation.summary: Adjunctive dexamethasone in adult bacterial meningitis reduces mortality and hearing loss specifically in pneumococcal disease. Timing matters: must be with or before the first antibiotic to blunt the cytokine surge from bacterial lysis.
key_points: 10 mg IV 6-hourly for 4 days
Give with or before first antibiotic
Continue only if pneumococcus confirmed; stop otherwise
Most evidence is for pneumococcal meningitis
explanation.pearls: The cytokine-surge logic means steroid given hours after antibiotic provides no benefit; treat the timing as a hard cutoff.

sources.label: de Gans, NEJM 2002 - Dexamethasone in adult meningitis

stem: A 74-year-old woman is admitted with a left middle cerebral artery territory ischaemic stroke. Echocardiogram and 72-hour cardiac monitor reveal paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. She has hypertension and previous transient ischaemic attack. Her CHA2DS2-VASc is 6. HAS-BLED is 2. She has no contraindication to anticoagulation. The stroke is moderate sized on MRI without haemorrhagic transformation.

data_table.Imaging: Moderate left MCA infarct, no haemorrhagic transformation on day 5 MRI.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate timing to start anticoagulation?

options.text: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily commenced on day 6 to 8 after stroke onset
options.rationale: The 1-3-6-12 day rule guides DOAC initiation post-stroke based on infarct size: 1 day for TIA, 3 days for small, 6 days for moderate, 12 days for large or haemorrhagic. Moderate stroke without haemorrhagic transformation supports day 6 to 8 initiation. Apixaban is preferred in elderly with renal-friendly profile (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022; eTG Cardiovascular).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily plus clopidogrel 75 mg daily long-term
options.rationale: Dual antiplatelet for AF-related stroke is inferior to anticoagulation and causes higher bleeding (ACTIVE-A; Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022). DAPT is for non-cardioembolic minor stroke/TIA short-term only.

options.text: Warfarin commenced immediately on day 1
options.rationale: Immediate anticoagulation in moderate or large infarct increases haemorrhagic conversion; staged initiation per infarct size is recommended. DOACs are preferred over warfarin in non-valvular AF (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily alone
options.rationale: Aspirin alone for AF stroke prevention is inferior to anticoagulation and is reserved for true anticoagulation contraindications (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Anticoagulation deferred until 6 weeks post-stroke
options.rationale: A 6-week delay leaves the patient unprotected during the highest-risk recurrence window; the 1-3-6-12 day rule is the AU pathway (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

explanation.summary: Anticoagulation timing after AF-related ischaemic stroke follows the 1-3-6-12 day rule based on infarct size. DOACs are first-line over warfarin in non-valvular AF. Bridging with aspirin is reasonable during the delay window.
key_points: TIA: day 1; small infarct: day 3; moderate: day 6; large/haemorrhagic: day 12
DOAC > warfarin in non-valvular AF
Apixaban preferred in elderly and renal impairment
Aspirin during the delay window is acceptable practice
explanation.pearls: ELAN trial (2023) supports earlier DOAC start than the 1-3-6-12 rule for some patients; guidelines updating gradually.

stem: A 68-year-old man presents to his general practitioner having had a 45-minute episode of right arm weakness and word-finding difficulty that resolved 3 hours ago. He has hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Blood pressure today is 162/94 and he is in sinus rhythm. Examination is now normal.
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Same-day specialist stroke clinic or emergency department referral for imaging and carotid assessment
options.rationale: ABCD2 >=4 indicates high short-term recurrent stroke risk and warrants same-day urgent assessment with brain imaging and carotid imaging. Even at lower ABCD2 scores, all suspected TIA warrants urgent assessment per Stroke Foundation. Carotid endarterectomy if symptomatic stenosis >=50% provides the largest benefit within 2 weeks (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Outpatient referral within 4 weeks
options.rationale: Delay risks early recurrent stroke, peak risk within 48 hours of TIA. Same-day assessment is the pathway (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg and review in 7 days
options.rationale: Aspirin alone underplays the urgency; dual antiplatelet (aspirin + clopidogrel) for 21 days is now standard for high-risk TIA, but requires same-day imaging to exclude haemorrhagic stroke first (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022; CHANCE/POINT).

options.text: Increase antihypertensive and review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: BP optimisation is part of secondary prevention but is not the immediate priority over imaging and antiplatelet initiation (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Reassurance as the episode has resolved
options.rationale: Resolved TIA carries 5 to 10% risk of stroke within 1 week; reassurance without workup is the classic anti-pattern (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

explanation.summary: TIA is a medical emergency. ABCD2 score helps risk-stratify but all TIA warrants urgent (same-day or within 24 hours) assessment with imaging, carotid evaluation, and initiation of dual antiplatelet therapy for high-risk TIA or minor stroke.
key_points: ABCD2 score: Age, BP, Clinical features, Duration, Diabetes
Same-day specialist assessment for all suspected TIA
DAPT (aspirin + clopidogrel) 21 days for high-risk TIA / minor stroke
Carotid intervention within 2 weeks if symptomatic stenosis >=50%
explanation.pearls: Highest stroke risk is in the first 48 hours; ABCD2 has been criticised as imperfect but the urgency message stands.

stem: A 62-year-old man presents 2 hours after sudden severe headache, vomiting, and left hemiparesis. Non-contrast CT brain shows a 12 mL right basal ganglia haemorrhage without intraventricular extension. He takes amlodipine for hypertension. He is on no antiplatelet or anticoagulant. Glasgow Coma Scale is 13.

data_table.Imaging: 12 mL right basal ganglia bleed, no IVH, no midline shift.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate blood pressure target and timeframe?

options.text: Systolic blood pressure 140 mmHg within 1 hour using titrated intravenous antihypertensive
options.rationale: INTERACT-2 and ATACH-II support intensive lowering to SBP <140 mmHg in acute spontaneous intracerebral haemorrhage within 6 hours of onset, reducing haematoma expansion. Use titrated IV agents (labetalol, GTN, clevidipine where available). Stroke Foundation endorses this target (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022; INTERACT-2).

options.text: Permissive hypertension - no antihypertensive treatment
options.rationale: Permissive hypertension applies to acute ischaemic stroke outside the lysis pathway; for spontaneous ICH the evidence supports active lowering to SBP <140 (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Systolic blood pressure <100 mmHg within 1 hour
options.rationale: Over-aggressive reduction risks cerebral hypoperfusion in the perihaematomal penumbra; the validated target is 140 not 100 (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022).

options.text: Sublingual nifedipine 10 mg
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine produces unpredictable hypotension and is contraindicated in acute stroke (eTG Cardiovascular).

options.text: Lower over the next 24 hours to <160 mmHg
options.rationale: Slow lowering misses the haematoma-expansion window which is greatest in the first 6 hours; the 1-hour target is the validated approach (Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines 2022; INTERACT-2).

explanation.summary: Acute spontaneous intracerebral haemorrhage benefits from intensive blood pressure lowering to SBP <140 mmHg within 1 hour, reducing haematoma expansion and improving functional outcome. Use titrated IV agents (labetalol, GTN); avoid sublingual nifedipine and over-aggressive reductions.
key_points: SBP target <140 mmHg within 1 hour
Window of greatest benefit is first 6 hours
Reverse anticoagulants (PCC + vitamin K for warfarin; specific reversal for DOACs)
Neurosurgical referral for cerebellar bleed, hydrocephalus, large lobar
explanation.pearls: INTERACT-2 was the practice-changing trial; ATACH-II tested lower targets without added benefit and signal of harm.

stem: A 26-year-old man presents to a metropolitan emergency department after a witnessed first generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 90 seconds. He bit his tongue and was post-ictal for 20 minutes. He has fully recovered. He drinks 4 standard drinks on Fridays and Saturdays. No illicit drug use. He works as a courier driver. Examination, electrolytes, glucose and ECG are normal. Non-contrast CT brain is normal.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78, BP 124/72, T 36.8 C, BSL 5.4 mmol/L.
data_table.Investigations: U&E, glucose, calcium, magnesium, CRP, ECG normal. CT brain normal.
data_table.Driving: Class C licence; works as a courier.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge with outpatient MRI brain and electroencephalogram (EEG), neurology follow-up within 4 weeks, no antiepileptic started, and advise non-driving per Austroads
options.rationale: After a first unprovoked seizure with normal examination, electrolytes and CT, the standard pathway is outpatient MRI and EEG to risk-stratify recurrence; antiepileptic medication is started only if recurrence risk is high (focal abnormality, epileptiform EEG) or after a second seizure. Austroads requires 6 months non-driving for private licence after first unprovoked seizure, 5 years for commercial (eTG Neurology; Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive 2022).

options.text: Start sodium valproate 500 mg orally twice daily and discharge with a 6-month GP follow-up, without outpatient MRI brain or electroencephalogram first
options.rationale: After a single unprovoked seizure with normal initial workup, antiepileptics are not routinely started; recurrence risk informs the decision (Source: eTG Neurology).

options.text: Admit for 48-hour inpatient video EEG monitoring as the next investigation, deferring outpatient MRI and outpatient neurology review until after admission
options.rationale: Inpatient video EEG is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty between epilepsy and non-epileptic events or for seizure characterisation, not routine first-seizure workup (Source: eTG Neurology).

options.text: Discharge with no neurology follow-up, no MRI, no EEG, and no specific driving advice provided that examination remains normal at the time of discharge
options.rationale: Failure to arrange neurology follow-up and imaging risks missing a structural lesion and leaves driving advice and recurrence-prevention unaddressed (Source: eTG Neurology; Austroads).

options.text: Perform a lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid analysis prior to discharge, despite the afebrile, alert and fully recovered status of the patient
options.rationale: Lumbar puncture is indicated only when meningitis, encephalitis or subarachnoid haemorrhage are clinically suggested; afebrile, alert, fully recovered patients do not need LP (Source: eTG Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: After a first unprovoked seizure, outpatient MRI brain and EEG inform recurrence risk and the decision to start antiepileptic. Provoked seizures (electrolyte derangement, withdrawal, fever) need treatment of the cause, not antiepileptic. Driving restrictions follow Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive.
key_points: MRI brain (higher yield than CT) and EEG outpatient
Antiepileptic starts at second seizure unless high-risk first-seizure features
Austroads: 6 months off private licence, 5 years commercial after first unprovoked
Educate on safety: no swimming alone, baths, heights, ladders
explanation.pearls: Mandatory reporting to driver licensing is the doctor's role in some jurisdictions; in NSW driver self-reports but doctor may notify if non-compliance puts public at risk (Austroads).

stem: A 19-year-old university student is admitted with confirmed invasive meningococcal disease (CSF and blood cultures positive for Neisseria meningitidis serogroup B). She lives in shared university accommodation with 4 housemates and attended a 5-hour party 2 days before admission with 12 other people. She is on appropriate antibiotics.

data_table.Contacts: 4 household contacts; 12 party attendees with prolonged close contact.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate public health response for close contacts?

options.text: Notify public health unit; offer ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally single dose (or rifampicin) to household and prolonged close contacts; arrange meningococcal B vaccination via public health
options.rationale: Invasive meningococcal disease is a notifiable disease in all Australian jurisdictions. Public health coordinates contact tracing and chemoprophylaxis (ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally single dose first-line; rifampicin or ceftriaxone alternatives) for household and prolonged close contacts within the previous 7 days. Vaccination of contacts is recommended for serogroup-specific exposure where vaccine is available (CDNA SoNG Meningococcal Disease).

options.text: Treat the index case only; contacts only need treatment if they develop symptoms
options.rationale: Chemoprophylaxis for close contacts within 7 days of index case onset reduces secondary cases; delaying until symptoms develop misses the prophylaxis window (CDNA SoNG).

options.text: Offer benzathine penicillin intramuscular to all contacts
options.rationale: Benzathine penicillin is not used for meningococcal prophylaxis; ciprofloxacin oral single dose is the AU first-line agent (eTG Antibiotic; CDNA SoNG).

options.text: Casual contacts (classmates, gym buddies) also require prophylaxis
options.rationale: Prophylaxis is for household, intimate, and prolonged close contacts only (>8 hours in confined space, sharing utensils, kissing). Casual contact does not warrant prophylaxis (CDNA SoNG).

options.text: Defer notification until microbiology confirms serogroup
options.rationale: Notification is on clinical suspicion or initial positive Gram stain/PCR - not after full serogroup characterisation. Delay forfeits prophylaxis window (Public Health Act NSW; CDNA SoNG).

explanation.summary: Invasive meningococcal disease is a notifiable urgent public health event. Chemoprophylaxis with single-dose ciprofloxacin for household, intimate, and prolonged close contacts within 7 days of onset reduces secondary cases. Public health coordinates vaccine offer for serogroups with available vaccines (B, ACWY).
key_points: Notify public health on clinical suspicion or initial positive Gram stain/PCR
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO single dose first-line (or rifampicin 600 mg BD x 2 days, or IM ceftriaxone in pregnancy)
Prophylaxis for household, intimate partners, prolonged close contacts (>8 h confined)
Vaccinate contacts for serogroup-specific exposure where vaccine available
explanation.pearls: The index patient also needs chemoprophylaxis prior to discharge unless treated with ceftriaxone (which eradicates carriage).

stem: A 64-year-old man presents with 4 months of progressive proximal lower limb weakness, dry mouth, erectile dysfunction, and difficulty rising from a chair. He has a 50 pack-year smoking history. On examination, knee reflexes are absent at rest but become elicitable after 10 seconds of sustained quadriceps contraction. Strength initially weak but improves with repeated effort. No ptosis, no ocular involvement.

data_table.Examination: Proximal LL weakness, areflexic at rest with post-exercise facilitation, no ocular involvement.
data_table.Autonomic: Dry mouth, ED, postural drop 22 mmHg.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Computed tomography (CT) thorax and voltage-gated calcium channel antibody assay
options.rationale: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS) is paraneoplastic in 50 to 60% (most often small cell lung cancer) and identified by post-exercise facilitation, autonomic features, and proximal weakness. Voltage-gated calcium channel (VGCC) antibodies are diagnostic; CT thorax screens for underlying malignancy (eTG Neurology; ANZAN).

options.text: Acetylcholine receptor antibody assay alone
options.rationale: AChR antibodies are for myasthenia gravis (postsynaptic); LEMS is presynaptic. Post-exercise facilitation, autonomic features and absent ocular involvement point to LEMS not MG (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Lumbar puncture for albuminocytological dissociation
options.rationale: Albuminocytological dissociation is for GBS; this presentation lacks ascending weakness and post-infectious context (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Magnetic resonance imaging spine
options.rationale: Spinal cord disease would produce a sensory level, bladder/bowel involvement and hyperreflexia; this is a neuromuscular junction picture (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Polysomnography
options.rationale: Sleep study does not address proximal weakness with autonomic features and post-exercise facilitation (eTG Neurology).

explanation.summary: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS) is a presynaptic neuromuscular junction disorder, often paraneoplastic (small cell lung cancer). Distinguishing features from MG: proximal weakness predominates over ocular/bulbar, autonomic features (dry mouth, ED), absent reflexes that facilitate with sustained contraction, and antibodies to voltage-gated calcium channels.
key_points: LEMS: presynaptic VGCC, proximal weakness, autonomic, post-exercise facilitation
MG: postsynaptic AChR, ocular/bulbar fatigability, autonomic intact
50-60% of LEMS is paraneoplastic - screen CT thorax for SCLC
Treatment: 3,4-diaminopyridine, immunotherapy, cancer treatment
explanation.pearls: Post-exercise facilitation (Lambert sign) is the bedside hallmark distinguishing LEMS from MG; reflexes return after 10-15 seconds of sustained contraction.
